questions.prompt: Quantities consumers are willing and able to buy at different prices.
questions.explanation: Demand is the schedule of quantities consumers are willing and able to buy at each price, ceteris paribus. The law of demand (inverse price-quantity relationship) holds because of substitution and income effects. Demand shifters include income, prices of related goods, consumer expectations, tastes, and the number of buyers. In AD-AS, aggregate demand (AD = C + I + G + NX) is the economy-wide demand curve; it slopes downward for different reasons — the real wealth effect, interest-rate effect, and open-economy effect.

questions.prompt: Model showing money, goods, services, and resources moving between households and firms.
questions.explanation: The circular flow model illustrates how households and firms interact in two markets: the product market (households buy goods/services, firms sell) and the factor market (households sell labor/land/capital, firms buy). The flow of dollars equals the flow of goods/resources in opposite directions. GDP can be measured as the total dollar flow in the product market (expenditure approach) or the total factor income (income approach). The model expands to include government (fiscal flows) and the financial sector (saving/investment) in AP Macro.

questions.prompt: Quantities producers are willing and able to sell at different prices.
questions.explanation: Supply is the schedule of quantities producers are willing and able to sell at each price, ceteris paribus. The law of supply (direct price-quantity relationship) reflects rising marginal costs. Supply shifters include input prices, technology, number of sellers, expectations, and government policies (taxes, subsidies). In AD-AS, the SRAS curve shows aggregate supply when input prices are sticky; LRAS is vertical at potential GDP (full employment). A positive supply shock shifts SRAS right, lowering the price level and raising real GDP.

questions.prompt: Economy combining markets and government intervention.
questions.explanation: A mixed economy combines private markets with government intervention. Markets allocate most goods via price signals (Adam Smith's 'invisible hand'), while government corrects market failures — providing public goods, regulating externalities, enforcing property rights, and redistributing income. The U.S., EU, and most modern economies are mixed; purely free-market or command economies are theoretical extremes. The degree of government involvement is a central question in AP Macro policy discussions.

questions.prompt: Choices that require giving up one option, benefit, or goal to get another.
questions.explanation: A trade-off is the situation in which gaining more of one good requires giving up some of another — the core consequence of scarcity. On the PPC, every efficient point represents a trade-off; moving along the curve increases one good only by decreasing the other. Trade-offs arise for households (leisure vs. income), governments (guns vs. butter), and central banks (inflation vs. unemployment on the short-run Phillips curve). Understanding trade-offs is the foundation of all AP Macro policy analysis.

questions.prompt: Next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made. On the PPC, the opportunity cost of producing one more unit of Good X is the quantity of Good Y sacrificed; because of the law of increasing opportunity cost, this ratio rises as production shifts. The Fisher equation and rational decision-making both depend on comparing opportunity costs at the margin. AP FRQs frequently ask students to calculate opportunity cost from a PPC table.

questions.prompt: Legal protections over ownership and use of resources, goods, or ideas.
questions.explanation: Property rights are legal guarantees allowing owners to use, transfer, or exclude others from resources and ideas. Strong, enforceable property rights lower transaction costs, encourage investment, and facilitate markets by giving producers confidence that returns on effort will not be seized. Nobel laureate Douglass North showed that institutional property rights are a primary driver of long-run economic growth. Weak property rights — common in command or transitional economies — reduce incentives and efficiency.

questions.prompt: Trade-off between maximizing total output and distributing benefits more fairly.
questions.explanation: Efficiency means maximizing total output from available resources (the economy is on, not inside, the PPC). Equity concerns the fairness of how that output is distributed. Policies promoting equity — progressive taxes, transfer payments, minimum wages — often reduce efficiency by altering incentives; policies maximizing efficiency (e.g., free markets) may leave some groups worse off. AP Macro uses this trade-off to evaluate fiscal and redistributive policies: a tax that corrects a market failure can improve both simultaneously.

questions.prompt: Model showing scarcity, opportunity cost, efficiency, and trade-offs.
questions.explanation: The PPC (also PPF) plots maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce with existing resources and technology. Points on the curve are efficient; inside = underutilized resources (recession); outside = currently unattainable. The curve's bowed-out shape reflects the law of increasing opportunity cost. Economic growth (more capital, better technology, more labor) shifts the PPC outward. AP FRQs regularly ask students to graph shifts, identify opportunity costs, and distinguish efficiency from attainability.

questions.prompt: Additional cost created by producing or choosing one more unit.
questions.explanation: The marginal cost (MC) of a decision is the additional cost incurred by producing or consuming one more unit. Rational agents choose actions where marginal benefit ≥ marginal cost — the equimarginal principle. On the PPC, MC of Good X is the Good Y sacrificed (rising due to increasing opportunity cost). In AD-AS analysis, a cost-push shock raises input costs, shifting SRAS left and increasing the price level — an economy-wide rise in marginal production costs. AP FRQs test MC reasoning in both micro and macro contexts.

questions.prompt: Focusing production on a narrower task or product.
questions.explanation: Specialization means concentrating production on goods where a producer has a comparative advantage (lowest opportunity cost). At the national level, specialization enables countries to consume beyond their own PPC when they trade. Adam Smith showed specialization within firms raises labor productivity through practice and division of labor. In AP Macro, specialization and trade together justify the gains illustrated by a consumption possibilities frontier that lies outside the PPC.

questions.prompt: Frontier model showing trade-offs and efficient combinations under resource limits.
questions.explanation: The CPF shows the combinations of two goods a country can consume after specializing and trading. With comparative advantage, the CPF lies outside the PPC along the good in which the country exports, allowing consumption impossible under autarky (self-sufficiency). The steeper or flatter slope of the CPF relative to the PPC's slope reveals the terms of trade. AP FRQs ask students to graph the CPF and identify the gains from trade.

questions.prompt: Ratio comparing export prices to import prices, showing how much imports a country can buy with its exports.
questions.explanation: Terms of trade (TOT) is the ratio of a country's export prices to its import prices (P_X / P_M); a rise means each unit of exports buys more imports, improving living standards. For two-country AP Macro problems, mutually beneficial trade requires that the agreed exchange ratio fall between each country's own opportunity-cost ratios. For example, if Country A's OC for wheat is 2 cloth and Country B's is 0.5 cloth, any ratio between 0.5 and 2 cloth per wheat makes both better off.

questions.prompt: Ability to produce at a lower opportunity cost.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage belongs to whoever can produce a good at the lowest opportunity cost — even if another producer is more efficient in absolute terms. David Ricardo's principle (1817) explains why trade is mutually beneficial: each country specializes in its comparative advantage good and trades for the other. AP FRQs provide a two-good, two-country output table; students calculate opportunity costs, identify who has comparative advantage in each good, and specify mutually beneficial terms of trade.

questions.prompt: Greater output with the same resources, distinct from lower opportunity cost.
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage means producing more output with the same inputs — or the same output with fewer inputs — than another producer. It differs critically from comparative advantage, which is based on lower opportunity cost. A country can have absolute advantage in everything yet still gain from trade by specializing where its comparative advantage lies. AP FRQs often give a table of outputs and ask students to distinguish absolute from comparative advantage, then identify mutually beneficial terms of trade.

questions.prompt: Condition where wants exceed available resources.
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the fundamental economic problem: unlimited human wants face limited resources (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship). Because no society can produce everything everyone desires, every economic system must answer the three core questions — what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom. On the PPC, any point on the curve reflects scarcity forcing a trade-off; a point inside the frontier is attainable but inefficient.

questions.prompt: An economy where government makes most economic decisions.
questions.explanation: In a command economy (central planning), the government owns the means of production and directs what, how, and for whom goods are produced, replacing price signals with bureaucratic allocation. The Soviet Union (1917–1991) is the canonical example. Command economies struggle with the 'knowledge problem' (Hayek): central planners cannot replicate the information embedded in millions of decentralized price signals, leading to chronic shortages, surpluses, and inefficiency. Most former command economies have transitioned to mixed systems.

questions.prompt: Principle that producing more of one good usually requires giving up increasing amounts of another.
questions.explanation: The law of increasing opportunity cost states that as more of one good is produced, each additional unit requires giving up increasingly larger amounts of the other good. This explains the bowed-out (concave) shape of the PPC: resources are not perfectly substitutable between uses, so shifting specialized labor and capital from one industry to another becomes progressively more costly. The principle generalizes to all resource allocation decisions: specialization is efficient up to a point, after which returns diminish.

questions.prompt: Rules and organizations, such as property rights, courts, banks, and firms, that structure economic activity.
questions.explanation: Economic institutions are the formal rules (laws, constitutions, contracts) and informal norms (customs, culture, trust) that structure economic interactions. Strong institutions — secure property rights, enforceable contracts, transparent courts, stable currency — lower transaction costs and encourage investment and specialization. Douglass North's 1993 Nobel Prize recognized that institutions, not just capital or technology, are the primary determinant of long-run economic performance. AP Macro contextualizes institutions as a determinant of LRAS and long-run growth.

questions.prompt: Choosing the option where expected marginal benefits are greater than expected marginal costs.
questions.explanation: Rational decision-making means choosing the option where expected marginal benefits exceed expected marginal costs, ignoring sunk costs and using only relevant (forward-looking) information. Economists assume rational agents, a key behavioral assumption underlying the AD-AS model, loanable funds market, and Phillips curve. Behavioral economics (Kahneman/Thaler) documents systematic deviations — loss aversion, anchoring, status quo bias — that AP Macro introduces as qualifications to pure rationality.

questions.prompt: Trade in which both sides choose to participate because each expects to gain.
questions.explanation: Voluntary exchange occurs when both parties to a transaction choose to participate because each expects to gain. This mutual-gain property means trade creates value without creating new resources — the basis for market economies. Prices emerge from voluntary exchange and signal information about relative scarcity. In the circular flow model, households voluntarily sell labor in factor markets and buy goods in product markets, generating GDP flows. Coerced exchange — taxation, regulation — is not voluntary by definition.

questions.prompt: Market outcome where planned buying equals planned selling.
questions.explanation: Market equilibrium occurs where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded — the price clears the market with no surplus or shortage. In AD-AS, short-run equilibrium is where AD intersects SRAS; if this differs from potential GDP (LRAS), a gap exists that either self-corrects (wages/prices adjust) or requires policy. Equilibrium real interest rate in the loanable funds market equals where saving (supply) meets investment demand. AP FRQs require identifying equilibrium, predicting shifts, and tracing to a new equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Government price ceilings or floors.
questions.explanation: Price controls are government-imposed limits on prices: a price ceiling (set below equilibrium) causes shortages — as with rent control — because QD > QS. A price floor (set above equilibrium) causes surpluses — as with minimum wage or agricultural price supports — because QS > QD. Both controls create deadweight loss (allocative inefficiency) and distort market signals. AP Macro uses price controls to illustrate government intervention trade-offs: equity goals can reduce efficiency.

questions.prompt: Additional benefit gained from one more unit of an action or good.
questions.explanation: Marginal benefit (MB) is the additional satisfaction or value gained from consuming or producing one more unit. Rational agents act when MB ≥ MC. The MB curve slopes downward (diminishing marginal utility): the first unit of a good provides more value than the tenth. In the loanable funds market, the demand-for-loanable-funds curve is effectively the MB curve for investment: firms borrow as long as the expected return (MB) exceeds the real interest rate (MC). AP FRQs test this reasoning in investment and fiscal policy contexts.

questions.prompt: Prices, profits, losses, rewards, or penalties that push buyers and sellers to change behavior.
questions.explanation: Market incentives are the price signals, profit opportunities, and cost pressures that guide decentralized economic decisions. Rising prices signal producers to supply more and consumers to buy less; falling prices do the reverse — Adam Smith's 'invisible hand.' Profits attract entry; losses drive exit. AP Macro examines how fiscal policy (taxes, subsidies) and monetary policy (interest-rate changes) alter incentives for consumption, investment, and saving, shifting AD and AS curves. Distorted incentives — from price controls or asymmetric information — reduce efficiency.

questions.prompt: GDP adjusted for inflation.
questions.explanation: Real GDP is nominal GDP adjusted for changes in the price level using a base-year price index (GDP deflator). The formula is: Real GDP = (Nominal GDP / GDP Deflator) × 100. Real GDP is the standard measure of economic output and growth because it removes inflation distortion; rising nominal GDP can mask falling real output if prices rise sharply. The BEA publishes real GDP quarterly; the Fed and Congress use it to assess the business cycle, output gaps, and policy needs.

questions.prompt: Sudden change in spending that shifts aggregate demand or market demand.
questions.explanation: A demand shock is a sudden, unexpected change in aggregate demand — the AD curve shifts left (negative) or right (positive). Positive demand shocks (pandemic stimulus checks, tax cuts, surging consumer confidence) raise price levels and real GDP in the short run. Negative demand shocks (financial crises, falling consumer confidence) lower both. The 2008 financial crisis was a severe negative demand shock; the 2020–21 fiscal/monetary response generated a strong positive demand shock. AD shifts are a core AP FRQ driver for the AD-AS model.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic curve showing output supplied when some input prices are sticky.
questions.explanation: The SRAS curve shows the total quantity of output supplied at each price level when input prices (especially wages) are sticky — they adjust slowly. SRAS slopes upward: higher output prices raise profit margins, inducing more production. A negative supply shock (oil price spike, supply chain disruption) shifts SRAS left, raising prices and reducing output simultaneously (stagflation). SRAS shifts right when input costs fall, productivity rises, or expectations improve. AP FRQs require drawing and shifting SRAS correctly in response to described events.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic curve showing full-employment output independent of the price level.
questions.explanation: The LRAS curve is vertical at potential GDP (full-employment output, Y*), the level of output when all resources are used at their natural rates. Because the price level does not affect long-run real output (money is neutral), LRAS is price-level independent. LRAS shifts right with increases in resources, technology, or productivity — long-run economic growth. AP FRQs use LRAS to define recessionary and inflationary gaps: a gap exists when AD-SRAS equilibrium differs from Y*; self-correction or policy closes the gap.

questions.prompt: High inflation and high unemployment at the same time.
questions.explanation: Stagflation combines stagnant output (recession/high unemployment) with high inflation — normally these move inversely on the short-run Phillips curve. It is caused by a negative supply shock that shifts SRAS left, raising the price level and lowering real GDP simultaneously. The 1970s U.S. oil embargoes (1973, 1979) are the definitive examples: energy prices spiked, SRAS shifted left, real GDP fell, and inflation rose above 10%. AP FRQs use stagflation to test students' ability to distinguish demand-side from supply-side causes in the AD-AS model.

questions.prompt: General rise in prices.
questions.explanation: Inflation is a sustained general increase in the price level, measured most commonly by the CPI (Consumer Price Index, published by BLS) or GDP deflator. Demand-pull inflation occurs when AD shifts right faster than SRAS/LRAS adjusts. Cost-push inflation (supply-push) occurs when SRAS shifts left. Inflation erodes the purchasing power of money and fixed incomes; unexpected inflation redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers (negative real interest rates). The Fed targets 2% annual inflation as measured by the PCE price index.

questions.prompt: Market value of final goods and services produced in a country.
questions.explanation: GDP is the market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given time period. Expenditure approach: GDP = C + I + G + (X − M). Income approach: GDP = wages + rents + interest + profits. Only final goods count (no double-counting intermediate goods). GDP excludes non-market activity (household production, black market), transfer payments, and financial transactions. Real GDP per capita is the standard proxy for standard of living. The BEA reports GDP quarterly; two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth define a technical recession.

questions.prompt: GDP measured in current prices.
questions.explanation: Nominal GDP measures total output at current market prices without adjusting for inflation. It will rise if prices rise even if real production is unchanged, making it a poor indicator of actual growth. To convert: Real GDP = (Nominal GDP / GDP Deflator) × 100. AP Macro routinely tests the distinction: if nominal GDP rises 8% and the price level rises 5%, real GDP rose only about 3%. The GDP deflator (Nominal GDP / Real GDP × 100) is a broad price index covering all domestically produced goods, unlike the CPI's fixed basket.

questions.prompt: Output gap where actual output is below potential output.
questions.explanation: A recessionary gap (negative output gap) exists when actual real GDP (the AD-SRAS intersection) is below potential GDP (LRAS). Unemployment exceeds the natural rate; the economy operates inside its PPC. Graphically, AD intersects SRAS to the left of LRAS. Self-correction: falling wages and input prices shift SRAS right until equilibrium reaches LRAS. Keynesian policy response: expansionary fiscal policy (↑G or ↓T) or expansionary monetary policy (↓interest rates) shifts AD right to close the gap faster. The 2008–2010 recession created a large recessionary gap.

questions.prompt: Unemployment from frictional and structural causes.
questions.explanation: The natural rate of unemployment (NRU) is the unemployment rate when the economy is at full employment — it equals frictional unemployment (workers between jobs) plus structural unemployment (skills/location mismatch), with no cyclical unemployment. Milton Friedman and Edmund Phelps introduced the concept to argue that attempting to keep unemployment below the NRU via expansionary policy causes accelerating inflation (the long-run Phillips curve is vertical). The U.S. CBO estimates NRU at roughly 4–5%; actual estimates shift with labor market changes.

questions.prompt: Government spending and taxation used to influence the economy.
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy is the use of government spending (G) and taxation (T) to influence macroeconomic conditions — output, employment, and inflation. Expansionary fiscal policy (↑G or ↓T) shifts AD right, raising real GDP and the price level; contractionary fiscal policy (↓G or ↑T) shifts AD left. The spending multiplier (1/[1−MPC]) amplifies initial changes. Fiscal policy is set by Congress and the President (legislative/executive) and operates with recognition, implementation, and impact lags. AP FRQs regularly require students to trace the AD-AS effects of a described fiscal action.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic curve showing total planned spending at different price levels.
questions.explanation: The AD curve plots the total quantity of real GDP demanded (C + I + G + NX) at each price level. It slopes downward for three reasons: (1) wealth/real balance effect — higher prices reduce real wealth, lowering C; (2) interest-rate effect — higher prices increase money demand, raising interest rates, reducing I; (3) open-economy effect — higher domestic prices make exports costlier, reducing NX. AD shifts (not moves along) when C, I, G, or NX change for non-price reasons. AP FRQs routinely require identifying what shifts AD and in which direction.

questions.prompt: Sudden change in production costs or availability.
questions.explanation: A supply shock is a sudden change in production costs or resource availability that shifts SRAS. A negative supply shock (oil price spike, pandemic supply chain collapse) shifts SRAS left, raising the price level and lowering real GDP — stagflation. A positive supply shock (technological breakthrough, falling energy costs) shifts SRAS right, lowering the price level and raising real GDP. Unlike demand shocks, supply shocks create a policy dilemma: fighting inflation requires contractionary policy that worsens output, while stimulating output worsens inflation.

questions.prompt: Share of the labor force unemployed and seeking work.
questions.explanation: The unemployment rate = (Unemployed / Labor Force) × 100, published monthly by the BLS. The labor force = employed + unemployed who are actively seeking work; it excludes discouraged workers and those not seeking employment. Three types: frictional (between jobs), structural (skills mismatch), and cyclical (business cycle). At full employment, only frictional and structural unemployment exist (the NRU). A falling unemployment rate in an expanding economy typically corresponds to moving along and then exceeding potential GDP in the AD-AS model.

questions.prompt: Output gap where actual output is above full-employment output.
questions.explanation: An inflationary gap (positive output gap) exists when actual real GDP (the AD-SRAS intersection) is above potential GDP (LRAS). The economy is temporarily producing beyond full capacity, driving unemployment below the NRU. Graphically, AD intersects SRAS to the right of LRAS. Self-correction: rising wages and input prices shift SRAS left until equilibrium returns to LRAS, raising the price level. Policy response: contractionary fiscal policy (↓G or ↑T) or contractionary monetary policy (↑interest rates) shifts AD left. The late 1990s U.S. boom created a mild inflationary gap.

questions.prompt: Expansion and contraction of economic activity over time.
questions.explanation: The business cycle describes the recurrent fluctuations of real GDP around its long-run trend: expansion (recovery through peak), contraction (recession through trough), and repeat. A recession is technically two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth. The NBER's Business Cycle Dating Committee officially identifies peaks and troughs. Unemployment rises during contractions (Okun's Law: each 1% rise in unemployment ≈ 2% fall in real GDP below potential). Fiscal and monetary policy aim to smooth the cycle, moderating both peaks and troughs.

questions.prompt: Unemployment caused by downturns in the business cycle.
questions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment results from downturns in the business cycle — it rises during recessions when AD falls and firms cut employment, and falls during expansions. It is the component of total unemployment that exceeds the NRU: cyclical unemployment = actual unemployment − NRU. Okun's Law quantifies the link: for each 1 percentage point of cyclical unemployment, real GDP falls about 2% below potential. Expansionary fiscal or monetary policy shifts AD right, reducing cyclical unemployment. At full employment (LRAS), cyclical unemployment = 0.

questions.prompt: Policies that change automatically with the economy.
questions.explanation: Automatic stabilizers are government spending and tax programs that expand/contract automatically with the business cycle without new legislation, dampening output swings. Examples: unemployment insurance rises during recessions (↑G), income tax revenues fall (↓T), and SNAP enrollment expands — all shift AD right without discretionary action. During expansions, the reverse occurs. Because they respond instantly, automatic stabilizers avoid the recognition and implementation lags of discretionary fiscal policy. They reduce, but do not eliminate, cyclical fluctuations.

questions.prompt: Low point of a business cycle before the economy begins to recover.
questions.explanation: The trough is the lowest point of a business cycle — the turning point between contraction and expansion. At the trough, real GDP is minimized, unemployment peaks, and capacity utilization is lowest. The economy operates well inside its LRAS (large recessionary gap). The NBER officially dates troughs; the Great Recession trough was June 2009, the COVID trough was April 2020 (the shortest recession on record). After the trough, recovery begins as AD strengthens and/or policy stimulus takes effect.

questions.prompt: High point of a business cycle before the economy begins to slow or contract.
questions.explanation: The peak is the highest point of economic activity in a business cycle — the turning point from expansion to contraction. At the peak, real GDP is maximized, unemployment is at or below the NRU, and inflationary pressures may be building (inflationary gap possible). The NBER officially dates peaks; the pre-2008 peak was December 2007, the pre-COVID peak was February 2020. After the peak, a contraction (recession) begins as AD weakens, investment falls, and/or monetary policy tightens.

questions.prompt: Share of adults working or actively seeking work.
questions.explanation: The labor force participation rate (LFPR) = (Labor Force / Civilian Non-institutional Population) × 100, published monthly by the BLS. It measures the share of the working-age population (16+, non-institutionalized) that is either employed or actively seeking work. A falling LFPR can mask labor market weakness: if discouraged workers stop searching, the unemployment rate falls even without new jobs. The U.S. LFPR peaked near 67% in 2000 and has trended lower since, partly reflecting Baby Boomer retirements and opioid crisis effects.

questions.prompt: Federal Reserve actions affecting money supply and interest rates.
questions.explanation: Monetary policy is the Federal Reserve's use of tools — open market operations (OMO), the federal funds rate target, the discount rate, and reserve requirements — to influence the money supply and interest rates. Expansionary (easy) monetary policy lowers interest rates, increasing investment (I) and consumption (C), shifting AD right. Contractionary (tight) policy raises interest rates, reducing borrowing, shifting AD left. The FOMC meets 8 times per year to set the federal funds rate target. AP FRQs require tracing the money market → loanable funds → AD-AS transmission chain.

questions.prompt: Measure of the price level based on a market basket.
questions.explanation: The CPI measures changes in the cost of a fixed basket of goods and services purchased by a typical urban consumer, compiled monthly by the BLS. Inflation rate = ([CPI_t − CPI_{t−1}] / CPI_{t−1}) × 100. The CPI overstates true inflation due to substitution bias (consumers switch to cheaper goods), new product bias, and quality improvement bias. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge is the PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures) index. AP FRQs use CPI to calculate inflation rates, compare nominal vs. real values, and evaluate monetary policy responses.

questions.prompt: Ability of a monetary authority to make policy decisions without short-term political pressure.
questions.explanation: Central bank independence means a monetary authority (like the Fed) can set policy without direct political interference from the executive or legislative branches. Independence is credited with lower and more stable inflation because it allows the central bank to make credible long-run commitments (e.g., 2% inflation target) without yielding to short-term pressures to over-stimulate. The Fed has instrument independence (chooses its tools and timing) but not goal independence (Congress has mandated the dual mandate: price stability + maximum employment). Erosion of independence typically raises inflation expectations.

questions.prompt: Government budget spending that lawmakers choose and adjust through annual appropriations.
questions.explanation: Discretionary spending refers to the portion of the government budget that Congress must explicitly appropriate each year — in contrast to mandatory spending (Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid) that flows automatically by law. Defense, education, infrastructure, and research are major discretionary categories. Changes in discretionary spending represent active (discretionary) fiscal policy, subject to lengthy legislative processes that create implementation lags. In the AD-AS model, a rise in discretionary government spending (G) shifts AD right by ΔG × the spending multiplier.

questions.prompt: Total spending on final goods and services at each price level.
questions.explanation: Aggregate demand (AD) is the total planned spending on final goods and services in an economy at each price level: AD = C + I + G + (X − M). The AD curve slopes downward (wealth effect, interest-rate effect, open-economy effect). AD shifts right when any component rises for non-price reasons (e.g., tax cuts raise C, lower interest rates raise I, government stimulus raises G, currency depreciation raises NX). AD shifts left during recessions, financial crises, or contractionary policy. Identifying the direction and size of AD shifts is the most frequently tested AD-AS skill on AP FRQs.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic curve showing total planned spending at different price levels.
questions.explanation: The AD curve plots the total quantity of real GDP demanded (C + I + G + NX) at each price level. It slopes downward for three reasons: (1) wealth/real balance effect — higher prices reduce real wealth, lowering C; (2) interest-rate effect — higher prices increase money demand, raising interest rates, reducing I; (3) open-economy effect — higher domestic prices make exports costlier, reducing NX. AD shifts (not moves along) when C, I, G, or NX change for non-price reasons. AP FRQs routinely require identifying what shifts AD and in which direction.

questions.prompt: Total production at each price level.
questions.explanation: Aggregate supply (AS) is the total quantity of real output producers are willing and able to supply at each price level. Short-run AS (SRAS) slopes upward because some input prices (wages, contracts) are sticky; Long-run AS (LRAS) is vertical at potential GDP (Y*) because all prices are flexible. Supply-side shocks, input cost changes, and productivity shifts move SRAS; changes in resources, technology, or institutions move LRAS. AP FRQs routinely distinguish SRAS shocks (short-run) from LRAS shifts (long-run growth).

questions.prompt: Aggregate supply when input prices are sticky.
questions.explanation: Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shows the positive relationship between the price level and real output when input prices are sticky. Firms supply more when output prices rise faster than (fixed) input costs, increasing profit margins. SRAS shifts left when input costs rise (negative supply shock, rising wages) and right when input costs fall or productivity rises. In the AD-AS model, the SR equilibrium is the AD-SRAS intersection; if this differs from LRAS (Y*), a gap exists that drives long-run adjustment via changing wages and prices.

questions.prompt: Sudden change in spending that shifts aggregate demand or market demand.
questions.explanation: A demand shock is a sudden, unexpected change in aggregate demand — the AD curve shifts left (negative) or right (positive). Positive demand shocks (pandemic stimulus checks, tax cuts, surging consumer confidence) raise price levels and real GDP in the short run. Negative demand shocks (financial crises, falling consumer confidence) lower both. The 2008 financial crisis was a severe negative demand shock; the 2020–21 fiscal/monetary response generated a strong positive demand shock. AD shifts are a core AP FRQ driver for the AD-AS model.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic curve showing output supplied when some input prices are sticky.
questions.explanation: The SRAS curve shows the total quantity of output supplied at each price level when input prices (especially wages) are sticky — they adjust slowly. SRAS slopes upward: higher output prices raise profit margins, inducing more production. A negative supply shock (oil price spike, supply chain disruption) shifts SRAS left, raising prices and reducing output simultaneously (stagflation). SRAS shifts right when input costs fall, productivity rises, or expectations improve. AP FRQs require drawing and shifting SRAS correctly in response to described events.

questions.prompt: Government spending and taxation used to influence the economy.
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy is the use of government spending (G) and taxation (T) to influence macroeconomic conditions — output, employment, and inflation. Expansionary fiscal policy (↑G or ↓T) shifts AD right, raising real GDP and the price level; contractionary fiscal policy (↓G or ↑T) shifts AD left. The spending multiplier (1/[1−MPC]) amplifies initial changes. Fiscal policy is set by Congress and the President (legislative/executive) and operates with recognition, implementation, and impact lags. AP FRQs regularly require students to trace the AD-AS effects of a described fiscal action.

questions.prompt: Measure of how a change in taxes affects total output through spending changes.
questions.explanation: The tax multiplier measures the impact of a lump-sum tax change on equilibrium real GDP: Tax Multiplier = −MPC / (1 − MPC) = −MPC × Spending Multiplier. It is negative (a tax cut raises GDP; a tax increase lowers it) and smaller in absolute value than the spending multiplier because some of a tax cut is saved (not spent). If MPC = 0.8, the tax multiplier = −4 and the spending multiplier = 5. AP FRQs may provide MPC and ask students to compute the change in GDP from a tax change, noting the multiplier is always 1 smaller in absolute value than the spending multiplier.

questions.prompt: Potential output when labor, capital, and technology are fully employed.
questions.explanation: Long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) is vertical at potential GDP (Y*) — the level of output when all resources are fully employed at their natural rates. In the long run, real output is determined by the stock of resources, technology, and productivity — not by the price level (money neutrality). LRAS shifts right with population growth, capital accumulation, technological improvement, or institutional improvements (economic growth). Self-correction: a recessionary gap → falling wages → SRAS shifts right → equilibrium returns to LRAS. AP FRQs distinguish LRAS growth from AD-driven short-run expansion.

questions.prompt: Lower spending or higher taxes to fight inflation.
questions.explanation: Contractionary fiscal policy uses decreases in government spending (↓G) or increases in taxes (↑T) to shift AD left, lowering the price level and cooling output — the prescription for an inflationary gap. Effects are amplified by the spending and tax multipliers. The 1990s Clinton-era budget surplus, the 2011 U.S. sequester cuts, and post-financial-crisis eurozone austerity are examples. Risk: if the economy weakens unexpectedly, contractionary policy can deepen a recession. AP FRQs require students to trace the AD-AS graph showing AD shifting left, price level falling, and real GDP falling.

questions.prompt: Federal Reserve actions affecting money supply and interest rates.
questions.explanation: Monetary policy is the Federal Reserve's use of tools — open market operations (OMO), the federal funds rate target, the discount rate, and reserve requirements — to influence the money supply and interest rates. Expansionary (easy) monetary policy lowers interest rates, increasing investment (I) and consumption (C), shifting AD right. Contractionary (tight) policy raises interest rates, reducing borrowing, shifting AD left. The FOMC meets 8 times per year to set the federal funds rate target. AP FRQs require tracing the money market → loanable funds → AD-AS transmission chain.

questions.prompt: Government borrowing effect that raises rates and reduces private investment.
questions.explanation: Crowding out occurs when government borrowing to finance a deficit raises real interest rates in the loanable funds market, reducing private investment (I) and partially offsetting the fiscal stimulus. In the loanable funds graph: ↑ government borrowing shifts the demand for loanable funds right → ↑ real interest rate → ↓ private investment. Complete crowding out (classical view) leaves GDP unchanged; partial crowding out (Keynesian view) reduces but doesn't eliminate the multiplier effect. AP FRQs require drawing the loanable funds market to illustrate crowding out.

questions.prompt: Output gap where actual output is below potential output.
questions.explanation: A recessionary gap (negative output gap) exists when actual real GDP (the AD-SRAS intersection) is below potential GDP (LRAS). Unemployment exceeds the natural rate; the economy operates inside its PPC. Graphically, AD intersects SRAS to the left of LRAS. Self-correction: falling wages and input prices shift SRAS right until equilibrium reaches LRAS. Keynesian policy response: expansionary fiscal policy (↑G or ↓T) or expansionary monetary policy (↓interest rates) shifts AD right to close the gap faster. The 2008–2010 recession created a large recessionary gap.

questions.prompt: Business purchases of capital goods plus residential construction and inventory changes.
questions.explanation: Investment (I) in GDP accounting = business purchases of capital goods (equipment, structures, software) + residential construction + changes in inventories. It is the most volatile component of GDP and is highly sensitive to real interest rates (the loanable funds channel): lower real rates → more investment → AD shifts right. A rise in business confidence or technological innovation also shifts I up, moving AD right. AP FRQs trace the monetary policy chain: Fed ↓ federal funds rate → ↓ real interest rate → ↑ investment → ↑ AD → ↑ real GDP, ↑ price level.

questions.prompt: Output gap where actual output is above full-employment output.
questions.explanation: An inflationary gap (positive output gap) exists when actual real GDP (the AD-SRAS intersection) is above potential GDP (LRAS). The economy is temporarily producing beyond full capacity, driving unemployment below the NRU. Graphically, AD intersects SRAS to the right of LRAS. Self-correction: rising wages and input prices shift SRAS left until equilibrium returns to LRAS, raising the price level. Policy response: contractionary fiscal policy (↓G or ↑T) or contractionary monetary policy (↑interest rates) shifts AD left. The late 1990s U.S. boom created a mild inflationary gap.

questions.prompt: Process where one change in spending creates a larger total change in real GDP.
questions.explanation: The spending multiplier = 1 / (1 − MPC) = 1 / MPS. An initial change in autonomous spending (ΔG, ΔI, ΔX) generates a larger total change in GDP because each dollar spent becomes income, a fraction (MPC) of which is spent again in successive rounds. If MPC = 0.75, multiplier = 4; so $100B in government spending raises GDP by $400B (before crowding-out adjustments). AP FRQs provide MPC or MPS and ask for the total GDP change from a fiscal action. The multiplier is smaller in open economies (some spending 'leaks' to imports) and when taxes are included.

questions.prompt: Household purchases of goods and services, the largest component of GDP.
questions.explanation: Consumption (C) is household purchases of goods and services — durable goods (cars, appliances), nondurables (food, clothing), and services (healthcare, housing). It is the largest component of U.S. GDP, typically 68–70%. The Keynesian consumption function: C = a + MPC × (Y − T), where 'a' is autonomous consumption and MPC is the marginal propensity to consume. A rise in disposable income, consumer confidence, or wealth shifts the consumption function up, increasing C and shifting AD right. AP FRQs use MPC to compute multipliers and trace fiscal policy effects on C.

questions.prompt: Government spending on goods and services counted directly in GDP.
questions.explanation: Government purchases (G) are government expenditures on goods and services — defense, roads, public schools, courts — that count directly in GDP = C + I + G + NX. Government transfer payments (Social Security, unemployment benefits) are NOT included in G because they are not purchases of current output. A rise in G shifts AD right by ΔG × spending multiplier. At the federal level, government purchases account for roughly 17–18% of U.S. GDP. AP FRQs distinguish G from transfers: only G directly enters the multiplier chain as autonomous spending.

questions.prompt: Increased spending or lower taxes to fight recession.
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy uses increases in government spending (↑G) or decreases in taxes (↓T) to shift AD right, raising real GDP and the price level. It is the Keynesian prescription for a recessionary gap. The spending multiplier (1/[1−MPC]) amplifies the initial ΔG; the tax multiplier (−MPC/[1−MPC]) amplifies ΔT. The 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act ($787B) and 2020–21 COVID relief packages are major U.S. examples. Downside: may crowd out private investment if financed by government borrowing, and adds to the national debt.

questions.prompt: Exports minus imports.
questions.explanation: Net exports (NX) = Exports (X) − Imports (M); it is the open-economy component of GDP = C + I + G + NX. NX > 0: trade surplus; NX < 0: trade deficit. NX is sensitive to exchange rates: a weaker domestic currency makes exports cheaper and imports dearer, increasing NX and shifting AD right. A stronger currency does the reverse. In the balance of payments, the current account's goods-and-services trade balance tracks NX. AP FRQs routinely link exchange rate changes to NX shifts and their AD-AS consequences.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic curve showing full-employment output independent of the price level.
questions.explanation: The LRAS curve is vertical at potential GDP (full-employment output, Y*), the level of output when all resources are used at their natural rates. Because the price level does not affect long-run real output (money is neutral), LRAS is price-level independent. LRAS shifts right with increases in resources, technology, or productivity — long-run economic growth. AP FRQs use LRAS to define recessionary and inflationary gaps: a gap exists when AD-SRAS equilibrium differs from Y*; self-correction or policy closes the gap.

questions.prompt: Difference between actual output and potential output.
questions.explanation: The output gap = Actual Real GDP − Potential GDP (Y*). A negative output gap (recessionary gap) means actual GDP < Y*: unemployment exceeds the NRU, resources are underutilized. A positive output gap (inflationary gap) means actual GDP > Y*: the economy overheats, pushing unemployment below NRU and fueling inflation. The CBO estimates the U.S. output gap quarterly. On the Phillips curve, a negative output gap corresponds to high unemployment and low inflation; a positive gap to low unemployment and rising inflation. Closing the output gap is the central goal of stabilization policy.

questions.prompt: Policies that change automatically with the economy.
questions.explanation: Automatic stabilizers are government spending and tax programs that expand/contract automatically with the business cycle without new legislation, dampening output swings. Examples: unemployment insurance rises during recessions (↑G), income tax revenues fall (↓T), and SNAP enrollment expands — all shift AD right without discretionary action. During expansions, the reverse occurs. Because they respond instantly, automatic stabilizers avoid the recognition and implementation lags of discretionary fiscal policy. They reduce, but do not eliminate, cyclical fluctuations.

questions.prompt: Use of fiscal or monetary policy to stabilize total spending, output, and employment.
questions.explanation: Demand management refers to the use of fiscal and/or monetary policy to stabilize aggregate demand — smoothing the business cycle by boosting AD in recessions and restraining it during inflationary expansions. Keynesian economics emphasizes active demand management; classical/supply-side economists argue it is ineffective or harmful due to lags, crowding out, and inflationary expectations. AP Macro frames this as the central debate in stabilization policy: the trade-offs between closing gaps quickly via policy versus allowing self-correction through wage/price flexibility.

questions.prompt: Amplified change in output from an initial spending change.
questions.explanation: The multiplier effect describes how an initial change in autonomous spending ripples through the economy, generating a larger total change in real GDP. Each round of spending becomes someone's income, a fraction (MPC) of which is spent again. The process continues until the sum converges: total ΔGDP = ΔSpending × [1/(1−MPC)]. With MPC = 0.8, a $1 injection raises GDP by $5. The multiplier is reduced by taxes (leakage), imports, saving, and crowding out. It operates on both demand-side (fiscal) and investment shocks, amplifying business cycle swings.

questions.prompt: Fraction of deposits a bank holds as reserves.
questions.explanation: The reserve ratio = Bank Reserves / Total Deposits. It has two components: the required reserve ratio (set by the Fed — now effectively 0%) and the excess reserve ratio (voluntary buffers held above requirements). The money multiplier = 1/reserve ratio; a higher ratio means less lending and a smaller multiplier. During the 2008 crisis, banks drastically raised their excess reserve ratio (parking trillions at the Fed), collapsing the effective multiplier and making quantitative easing less inflationary than feared. AP FRQs test money multiplier calculations using the reserve ratio.

questions.prompt: Portion of deposits banks must hold rather than lend out.
questions.explanation: Required reserves = Required Reserve Ratio × Total Deposits. They are the legally mandated minimum reserves a bank must hold. A $1,000 deposit with RRR = 10% requires $100 in required reserves, leaving $900 as loanable (potential excess) reserves. The Fed can change RRR to alter the money multiplier, but this tool is rarely used (and since 2020 the formal RRR is 0% in the U.S.). Required reserves do not earn the bank a return (traditionally) but ensure a baseline of liquidity. AP FRQs often ask students to calculate required reserves and the maximum money creation from a new deposit.

questions.prompt: Amount of money circulating in the economy.
questions.explanation: The money supply (MS) is the total amount of money in circulation, measured by M1 or M2 aggregates. The Fed controls MS primarily via open market operations: buying Treasury securities injects reserves → banks lend more → MS expands by the money multiplier (1/required reserve ratio). Selling securities contracts MS. Money supply is represented as a vertical line in the money market graph (the Fed sets it independently of the interest rate). An increase in MS lowers the nominal interest rate, reducing real rates and stimulating investment and AD.

questions.prompt: Legal minimum share of deposits banks must hold as reserves.
questions.explanation: The reserve requirement (required reserve ratio, RRR) is the legal minimum fraction of deposits that commercial banks must hold as reserves (vault cash or deposits at the Fed). If RRR = 10%, a bank receiving $1,000 must keep $100 and can lend $900, which multiplies through the system. Raising RRR contracts the money multiplier and MS (contractionary); lowering it expands both (expansionary). In 2020, the Fed set reserve requirements to 0% — banks now hold reserves voluntarily. AP FRQs still test the RRR concept as a theoretical policy tool.

questions.prompt: Financial institutions that accept deposits, make loans, and help create money through lending.
questions.explanation: Commercial banks are depository institutions that accept deposits, extend loans, and create money through the fractional reserve system. When a bank receives $1,000 in deposits and holds 10% as required reserves, it lends $900, which is re-deposited elsewhere, and so on — creating money up to the money multiplier (1/RRR = 10×). Commercial banks profit from the spread between loan interest rates and deposit rates. Bank failures reduce MS sharply; FDIC deposit insurance (up to $250,000/account since 2008) prevents bank runs by protecting depositors.

questions.prompt: Expansion of the money supply when banks lend excess reserves through the banking system.
questions.explanation: Money creation occurs when commercial banks lend out a portion of deposits, which are then re-deposited and re-lent in successive rounds — the money multiplier process. Starting from new reserves (ΔR), the maximum new money created = ΔR × (1/RRR). The Fed injects reserves primarily by purchasing Treasury securities (OMO), enabling banks to expand loans and MS. The 2008 financial crisis showed this in reverse: as banks built up excess reserves and lending collapsed, the money multiplier plummeted despite massive Fed injections (quantitative easing).

questions.prompt: Federal Reserve buying or selling securities.
questions.explanation: Open market operations (OMO) are the Fed's primary monetary policy tool: the FOMC directs the New York Fed to buy or sell U.S. Treasury securities in the open market. Buying securities injects reserves into the banking system → MS increases → federal funds rate falls → real interest rates fall → investment and consumption rise → AD shifts right (expansionary). Selling securities has the opposite effect (contractionary). OMO are flexible, reversible, and effective — the daily mechanism the Fed uses to hit its federal funds rate target.

questions.prompt: Government protection of bank deposits that reduces panic and bank-run risk.
questions.explanation: Deposit insurance is a government guarantee — in the U.S., the FDIC (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, est. 1933) insures deposits up to $250,000 per depositor per institution. It prevents bank runs by ensuring depositors won't lose their savings if a bank fails, maintaining confidence in the financial system. Without deposit insurance, fear of insolvency causes rational bank runs (self-fulfilling), collapsing the banking system. The FDIC is funded by premiums paid by member banks, not taxpayer money. Deposit insurance is a cornerstone of financial sector stability in AP Macro.

questions.prompt: Deposit expansion ratio based on reserve requirements and bank lending.
questions.explanation: The money multiplier = 1 / Reserve Ratio (RR). It quantifies how much the money supply expands per dollar of new bank reserves. If RR = 0.10, the multiplier = 10: a $1,000 deposit can ultimately create up to $10,000 in new deposits. In practice, the actual multiplier is lower because banks hold excess reserves and some cash leaks out of the banking system. The Fed can shrink the effective multiplier by raising the required reserve ratio (rare) or by paying interest on excess reserves (as during 2008–2015). AP FRQs require computing the maximum change in MS from a new deposit or Fed OMO.

questions.prompt: Banking system where banks hold part of deposits and lend the rest.
questions.explanation: Fractional reserve banking means banks hold only a fraction of deposits as reserves (required + excess) and lend the rest, creating new deposits (money) in the process. If the reserve ratio = 10%, a $1,000 deposit supports up to $9,000 in new loans. This creates the money multiplier but also makes the system vulnerable to bank runs: if many depositors withdraw simultaneously, banks may not have sufficient reserves. Deposit insurance (FDIC), reserve requirements, and the Fed as lender of last resort stabilize the system.

questions.prompt: Macroeconomic graph showing money supply, money demand, and the equilibrium nominal interest rate.
questions.explanation: The money market graph plots the nominal interest rate on the vertical axis against the quantity of money on the horizontal axis. Money supply (MS) is a vertical line (set by the Fed via OMO); money demand (MD) slopes downward (lower interest rates → more money held for transactions). The equilibrium nominal interest rate is where MS intersects MD. Expansionary monetary policy shifts MS right → lower nominal interest rate → lower real rate via Fisher equation → more investment → AD shifts right. AP FRQs require drawing this graph and linking the interest rate change to the loanable funds market and AD-AS.

questions.prompt: Governing body of the U.S. central bank system that helps set monetary policy.
questions.explanation: The Federal Reserve Board of Governors is a 7-member panel appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate, serving staggered 14-year terms to insulate monetary policy from political pressure. The Chair (currently leading policy decisions) has a 4-year term and is the Fed's public face. The Board oversees the 12 regional Federal Reserve Banks, sets reserve requirements, and, along with regional bank presidents, forms the 12-member FOMC — the committee that votes on the federal funds rate target 8 times per year.

questions.prompt: Central bank of the United States.
questions.explanation: The Federal Reserve ('the Fed') is the U.S. central bank, established in 1913. Its structure: 12 regional Federal Reserve Banks overseen by a 7-member Board of Governors (appointed by the President, confirmed by Senate). The FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) sets monetary policy. Dual mandate: price stability (≈2% inflation target) and maximum employment. Primary tools: open market operations (most powerful), federal funds rate targeting, discount rate, and reserve requirements. The Fed also regulates banks, provides payment services, and acts as lender of last resort.

questions.prompt: Stated interest rate before adjusting for inflation.
questions.explanation: The nominal interest rate is the stated rate of return on a loan or investment before adjusting for inflation. Fisher equation: r = i − π, where r = real interest rate, i = nominal interest rate, π = inflation rate. Equivalently: i = r + π. When inflation rises unexpectedly, real interest rates fall even if nominal rates are unchanged — redistributing wealth from lenders (who receive less real value) to borrowers. The federal funds rate and discount rate are quoted as nominal rates. AP FRQs test the Fisher equation to distinguish nominal from real effects of monetary policy.

questions.prompt: Ability of a monetary authority to make policy decisions without short-term political pressure.
questions.explanation: Central bank independence means a monetary authority (like the Fed) can set policy without direct political interference from the executive or legislative branches. Independence is credited with lower and more stable inflation because it allows the central bank to make credible long-run commitments (e.g., 2% inflation target) without yielding to short-term pressures to over-stimulate. The Fed has instrument independence (chooses its tools and timing) but not goal independence (Congress has mandated the dual mandate: price stability + maximum employment). Erosion of independence typically raises inflation expectations.

questions.prompt: Medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value.
questions.explanation: Money serves three functions: (1) medium of exchange — eliminates barter's double coincidence of wants problem; (2) unit of account — common measure of value; (3) store of value — retains purchasing power over time. M1 (narrowest): currency + demand deposits + traveler's checks. M2: M1 + savings accounts + small CDs + money market funds. Commodity money has intrinsic value (gold); fiat money has value by government decree and public confidence. The Fed controls the money supply primarily through open market operations, the discount rate, and reserve requirements.

questions.prompt: Deposits banks hold rather than lend.
questions.explanation: Bank reserves are the funds banks hold either as vault cash or as deposits at Federal Reserve Banks — not lent out. Required reserves = RRR × deposits. Excess reserves = actual reserves − required reserves (available for lending). When the Fed buys Treasury securities via OMO, it credits bank reserve accounts, increasing their capacity to lend and expanding MS. During 2008–2015, the Fed paid interest on excess reserves (IOER) to incentivize banks to hold extra reserves rather than lend, providing a floor under the federal funds rate.

questions.prompt: Interest rate banks charge each other for overnight loans.
questions.explanation: The federal funds rate is the interest rate at which banks lend reserve balances to each other overnight. The FOMC sets a target (or target range) for this rate, which anchors short-term interest rates throughout the economy. Lowering the target → cheaper bank borrowing → lower rates on auto loans, mortgages, business loans → ↑C and ↑I → AD shifts right (expansionary). Raising the target does the reverse. The Fed implements its target via OMO: buying securities to inject reserves (push rate down) or selling to drain reserves (push rate up). The effective federal funds rate closely tracks the target.

questions.prompt: Bank reserves above the required amount that can support additional lending.
questions.explanation: Excess reserves = actual bank reserves − required reserves. They represent the additional lending capacity banks choose not to deploy. In normal times, banks minimize excess reserves (idle money earns nothing). After 2008, the Fed began paying interest on excess reserves (IOER/IOR), and banks accumulated trillions in excess reserves at the Fed — limiting money multiplier expansion despite massive quantitative easing. Excess reserves are a key reason the money multiplier is lower in practice than the theoretical 1/RRR maximum. AP FRQs test the effect of excess reserves on the money creation process.

questions.prompt: Market values of debt securities that move inversely with interest rates.
questions.explanation: Bond prices and interest rates move inversely: when interest rates rise, existing bonds paying lower fixed coupons become less attractive, so their market prices fall; when rates fall, existing bonds rise in price. This is mechanical: Price = Coupon / Interest Rate (simplified). The Fed's OMO exploits this: buying bonds in the open market bids up bond prices → lower interest rates → expansionary. Selling bonds pushes prices down → higher interest rates → contractionary. AP FRQs test whether students correctly identify the direction of bond-price change when the Fed acts.

questions.prompt: Government borrowing effect that raises rates and reduces private investment.
questions.explanation: Crowding out occurs when government borrowing to finance a deficit raises real interest rates in the loanable funds market, reducing private investment (I) and partially offsetting the fiscal stimulus. In the loanable funds graph: ↑ government borrowing shifts the demand for loanable funds right → ↑ real interest rate → ↓ private investment. Complete crowding out (classical view) leaves GDP unchanged; partial crowding out (Keynesian view) reduces but doesn't eliminate the multiplier effect. AP FRQs require drawing the loanable funds market to illustrate crowding out.

questions.prompt: Interest rate the Fed charges banks for loans.
questions.explanation: The discount rate is the interest rate the Federal Reserve charges commercial banks for short-term loans through the discount window. Lowering the discount rate encourages banks to borrow reserves from the Fed → MS expands (expansionary); raising it discourages borrowing (contractionary). The discount rate is set above the federal funds rate to discourage routine borrowing — banks prefer to borrow from each other (fed funds market) first. During the 2008 crisis, the Fed cut the discount rate and broadened discount window access to stabilize the financial system.

questions.prompt: Interest rate adjusted for inflation, showing the real cost of borrowing.
questions.explanation: The real interest rate adjusts the nominal rate for inflation: r = i − π (Fisher equation). It represents the true cost of borrowing and the true return on saving in terms of purchasing power. Investment decisions are made based on the real interest rate: if r = 2%, the expected real return on capital must exceed 2% to justify investment. The loanable funds market determines the equilibrium real interest rate where saving (supply) equals investment demand. AP FRQs trace how fiscal deficits raise real rates (crowding out) or how Fed policy lowers nominal and real rates.

questions.prompt: Model of saving, borrowing, and real interest rates.
questions.explanation: The loanable funds market model determines the equilibrium real interest rate where desired saving (supply of loanable funds) equals desired investment (demand for loanable funds). Supply comes from household saving, government saving (surplus), and foreign capital inflows. Demand comes from business investment, government borrowing (deficit), and net foreign investment. A government deficit increases demand → real rate rises → private investment falls (crowding out). Expansionary monetary policy increases the supply of loanable funds → lower real rate → more investment → AD shifts right. AP FRQs require drawing and shifting this market.

questions.prompt: Current worth of a future payment after discounting it by an interest rate.
questions.explanation: Present value (PV) is the current worth of a future sum of money, discounted at the relevant interest rate: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n. Higher interest rates lower the present value of future returns, reducing investment's attractiveness. This is the mechanism behind the loanable funds model's downward-sloping investment demand curve: as real rates rise, the PV of future project revenues falls, making fewer projects profitable. AP Macro uses PV reasoning to explain why bond prices fall when interest rates rise and why investment is interest-rate sensitive.

questions.prompt: Vertical model showing no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment at the natural rate.
questions.explanation: The long-run Phillips curve (LRPC) is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment (NRU), reflecting the absence of a permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment. Friedman and Phelps (1968) showed that attempts to maintain unemployment below the NRU via demand expansion generate accelerating inflation (adaptive expectations adjust upward), eventually returning unemployment to the NRU at a higher inflation rate. Each attempt to push below NRU shifts the SRPC up. The LRPC illustrates money neutrality in the long run: more money → more inflation, not less unemployment.

questions.prompt: Model showing an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment in the short run.
questions.explanation: The short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment: when unemployment falls (economy expands), inflation rises, and vice versa. The SRPC shifts when inflation expectations change or supply shocks occur. An upward shift (stagflation) means both inflation and unemployment are higher at every point — as happened in the 1970s. A downward shift means lower inflation at every unemployment rate — as occurred in the 1990s with anchored expectations. The SRPC is linked to AD-AS: moving along AD corresponds to moving along the SRPC.

questions.prompt: Government borrowing effect that raises rates and reduces private investment.
questions.explanation: Crowding out occurs when government borrowing to finance a deficit raises real interest rates in the loanable funds market, reducing private investment (I) and partially offsetting the fiscal stimulus. In the loanable funds graph: ↑ government borrowing shifts the demand for loanable funds right → ↑ real interest rate → ↓ private investment. Complete crowding out (classical view) leaves GDP unchanged; partial crowding out (Keynesian view) reduces but doesn't eliminate the multiplier effect. AP FRQs require drawing the loanable funds market to illustrate crowding out.

questions.prompt: Loanable-funds graph showing government borrowing raising real interest rates and reducing private investment.
questions.explanation: The crowding-out effect is illustrated using the loanable funds graph: when the government runs a deficit and borrows, it increases the demand for loanable funds (the demand curve shifts right). This drives up the equilibrium real interest rate, which reduces private investment spending — partly offsetting the expansionary fiscal stimulus. Graphically: D_LF shifts right → higher real interest rate (r*) → lower investment. If the crowding out is complete (classical view), the increase in G equals the decrease in I and GDP is unchanged. AP FRQs frequently require this graph alongside the AD-AS graph.

questions.prompt: Model of saving, borrowing, and real interest rates.
questions.explanation: The loanable funds market model determines the equilibrium real interest rate where desired saving (supply of loanable funds) equals desired investment (demand for loanable funds). Supply comes from household saving, government saving (surplus), and foreign capital inflows. Demand comes from business investment, government borrowing (deficit), and net foreign investment. A government deficit increases demand → real rate rises → private investment falls (crowding out). Expansionary monetary policy increases the supply of loanable funds → lower real rate → more investment → AD shifts right. AP FRQs require drawing and shifting this market.

questions.prompt: Unemployment from frictional and structural causes.
questions.explanation: The natural rate of unemployment (NRU) is the unemployment rate when the economy is at full employment — it equals frictional unemployment (workers between jobs) plus structural unemployment (skills/location mismatch), with no cyclical unemployment. Milton Friedman and Edmund Phelps introduced the concept to argue that attempting to keep unemployment below the NRU via expansionary policy causes accelerating inflation (the long-run Phillips curve is vertical). The U.S. CBO estimates NRU at roughly 4–5%; actual estimates shift with labor market changes.

questions.prompt: Model showing short-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment.
questions.explanation: The Phillips curve, first described by A.W. Phillips (1958) using UK data, showed an inverse short-run relationship between wage inflation (and later price inflation) and unemployment. The SRPC is downward sloping: lower unemployment → higher inflation. The LRPC is vertical at the NRU — no long-run trade-off. Expectation-augmented Phillips curve: actual inflation = expected inflation + a(NRU − actual unemployment). AP FRQs use the Phillips curve to explain the consequences of stabilization policy, supply shocks, and changing inflation expectations.

questions.prompt: Lower spending or higher taxes used to fight inflation.
questions.explanation: Contractionary fiscal policy uses decreases in government spending (↓G) or increases in taxes (↑T) to shift AD left, lowering the price level and cooling output — the prescription for an inflationary gap. Effects are amplified by the spending and tax multipliers. The 1990s Clinton-era budget surplus, the 2011 U.S. sequester cuts, and post-financial-crisis eurozone austerity are examples. Risk: if the economy weakens unexpectedly, contractionary policy can deepen a recession. AP FRQs require students to trace the AD-AS graph showing AD shifting left, price level falling, and real GDP falling.

questions.prompt: Sudden change in production costs or availability.
questions.explanation: A supply shock is a sudden change in production costs or resource availability that shifts SRAS. A negative supply shock (oil price spike, pandemic supply chain collapse) shifts SRAS left, raising the price level and lowering real GDP — stagflation. A positive supply shock (technological breakthrough, falling energy costs) shifts SRAS right, lowering the price level and raising real GDP. Unlike demand shocks, supply shocks create a policy dilemma: fighting inflation requires contractionary policy that worsens output, while stimulating output worsens inflation.

questions.prompt: Public trust that a central bank will keep inflation and policy commitments believable.
questions.explanation: Central bank credibility refers to the public's trust that the central bank will honor its stated inflation target and not succumb to political pressure to over-stimulate. High credibility anchors inflation expectations (π^e) near the target, shifting the SRPC downward and making disinflation less costly (less unemployment). The Fed established credibility through Volcker's aggressive disinflation (1979–1983). Credibility is maintained by transparency (forward guidance), operational independence, and consistent policy. Loss of credibility (e.g., excessive money printing) risks unanchoring expectations and requires painful recessions to restore.

questions.prompt: Government spending greater than revenue.
questions.explanation: A budget deficit occurs when government expenditures (G + transfers + interest) exceed tax revenues (T) in a given year: Deficit = G − T > 0. Deficits are typically countercyclical — rising in recessions (automatic stabilizers) and shrinking in expansions. Persistent deficits accumulate into the national debt. Deficit spending shifts AD right (expansionary), but borrowing to finance it can crowd out private investment via rising real interest rates. The U.S. federal deficit peaked at roughly $3.1 trillion in FY2020 (COVID response). AP FRQs distinguish deficits (flow) from debt (stock).

questions.prompt: Output per unit of input.
questions.explanation: Productivity is output per unit of input — most commonly labor productivity (GDP per worker hour) or total factor productivity (TFP, efficiency of all inputs). Productivity growth is the primary driver of long-run real GDP growth and rising living standards. The Solow growth model attributes long-run growth to capital accumulation and, fundamentally, technological progress (rising TFP). Productivity growth shifts LRAS right without inflation pressure. AP FRQs link government policies (education spending, R&D subsidies, infrastructure) to productivity gains and long-run growth.

questions.prompt: Higher spending or lower taxes used to fight recession.
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy uses increases in government spending (↑G) or decreases in taxes (↓T) to shift AD right, raising real GDP and the price level. It is the Keynesian prescription for a recessionary gap. The spending multiplier (1/[1−MPC]) amplifies the initial ΔG; the tax multiplier (−MPC/[1−MPC]) amplifies ΔT. The 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act ($787B) and 2020–21 COVID relief packages are major U.S. examples. Downside: may crowd out private investment if financed by government borrowing, and adds to the national debt.

questions.prompt: Accumulated government borrowing.
questions.explanation: The national debt is the accumulated stock of all past government budget deficits minus surpluses — the total amount the federal government owes to domestic and foreign creditors. The U.S. national debt exceeded $34 trillion by 2024, roughly 120% of GDP. A high debt-to-GDP ratio can raise concerns about future tax burdens, crowd out private investment (higher real interest rates), and limit fiscal policy flexibility in future recessions. However, debt held in the country's own currency presents different risks than foreign-currency debt (as seen in emerging-market crises).

questions.prompt: Total amount the government owes from past borrowing.
questions.explanation: Public debt (synonymous with national debt in the U.S. context) is the total outstanding borrowing of the federal government. It equals the sum of all annual budget deficits minus surpluses. Debt held by the public (bonds sold to individuals, banks, foreign governments) drives macroeconomic effects; intragovernmental debt (Social Security trust fund bonds) is an internal accounting item. High public debt can raise real interest rates, crowd out private investment, and create solvency risks. AP Macro distinguishes debt (stock) from deficit (annual flow): deficit = change in debt.

questions.prompt: Skills, education, and health of workers.
questions.explanation: Human capital is the stock of productive knowledge, skills, health, and capabilities embodied in workers — accumulated through education, training, and on-the-job experience. Human capital raises worker productivity and wages, shifting LRAS right and driving long-run growth. Unlike physical capital, human capital cannot be separated from the worker. AP Macro treats education subsidies, job training programs, and healthcare investments as supply-side policies that build human capital and expand potential GDP. Nobel laureate Gary Becker pioneered the formal analysis of human capital investment decisions.

questions.prompt: Beliefs about future price increases that influence wages, prices, interest rates, and spending.
questions.explanation: Inflation expectations (π^e) are households', firms', and workers' beliefs about future inflation rates. They are self-fulfilling: workers who expect 5% inflation demand 5% wage increases, raising firms' costs and causing 5% inflation. Expectations shift the SRPC: higher π^e shifts the SRPC up (higher inflation at every unemployment rate); lower π^e shifts it down. The Fed manages expectations through transparent forward guidance and credible inflation targeting (currently 2% PCE). Anchored expectations reduce the sacrifice ratio (output loss per point of disinflation).

questions.prompt: Tools, machines, and infrastructure used in production.
questions.explanation: Physical capital includes man-made resources used in production: machinery, equipment, buildings, infrastructure, and software. Capital accumulation — investing more than depreciation — increases the capital stock, raising productivity and shifting LRAS right. The Solow model shows diminishing returns to capital: rich countries gain less per dollar of new capital than poor countries (conditional convergence). Government policies promoting physical capital investment (investment tax credits, infrastructure spending, lower real interest rates) are supply-side growth strategies. AP FRQs may ask how capital accumulation affects the PPC or LRAS.

questions.prompt: High inflation and high unemployment at the same time.
questions.explanation: Stagflation combines stagnant output (recession/high unemployment) with high inflation — normally these move inversely on the short-run Phillips curve. It is caused by a negative supply shock that shifts SRAS left, raising the price level and lowering real GDP simultaneously. The 1970s U.S. oil embargoes (1973, 1979) are the definitive examples: energy prices spiked, SRAS shifted left, real GDP fell, and inflation rose above 10%. AP FRQs use stagflation to test students' ability to distinguish demand-side from supply-side causes in the AD-AS model.

questions.prompt: Improvement in knowledge, tools, or methods that lets an economy produce more efficiently.
questions.explanation: Technological progress (advances in knowledge, methods, and tools) is the ultimate driver of sustained long-run economic growth in the Solow model — without it, capital accumulation leads to a steady state with zero growth per capita. Technology shifts LRAS right and the PPC outward without inflation pressure. AP Macro links R&D spending, intellectual property protection, and education to technological innovation.
